sections.title: The Unfinished Arrival
text: You wake on cold stone in a ruin that feels half-written.
The ceiling arches like a sentence that never found its end.
When you push yourself up, something sharp bites your palm—small, petty pain with a big opinion.

lines: Your HP dips—temporary, measurable. But beneath it, something else shifts, quieter. Health is not a number you can bluff.

sections.title: Details Others Miss
text: The air smells like old rain and older decisions.
Your breath fogs briefly, then fades—like the world is conserving effort.

lines: With a little attention, you notice scuffs in the dust—recent. Confident footsteps.
That confidence has ended many stories early.
It’s quiet. Quiet enough to feel safe.
That is a common mistake.

choices.text: Take the narrow gap before it closes.

sections.title: The Weight of Moving On
text: The corridor slopes downward. The stone sweats. The floor is uneven in the way that punishes ankles and pride.
Halfway through, the ceiling dips. You have to crouch—then crawl—then decide how badly you want to keep going.

lines: You grit your teeth and keep your rhythm. The strain stays small—paid, but affordable.
You make it through, but every movement feels like bargaining.
You win. You just don’t win clean.

choices.text: Push forward before the stone changes its mind.

sections.title: Too Slow or Too Careful
text: Ahead, a doorframe is split by a hairline crack of light—thin, living.
Something on the other side shifts. The crack narrows, as if the world is blinking.

lines: Your body understands the moment before your thoughts do. You’re already moving.
You hesitate. Not long—just long enough for the world to notice you negotiating with yourself.

choices.text: Slip through the crack while it’s still there.

lines: By the time you commit, the opening is less forgiving. So is the floor.

sections.title: Luck Does Not Explain Itself
text: The next chamber is a dead-end—except for a pedestal holding a small clay bowl.
Inside: a folded strip of parchment and a single coin that looks too new to belong here.

lines: The coin flips in your fingers like it wants attention.
It lands on its edge. Of course it does.
That shouldn’t have worked. Let’s not talk about it.
The coin slips. The bowl cracks. The room seems… pleased.
Luck isn’t always a gift. Sometimes it’s just a decision the world makes without you.

choices.text: Leave it. Walk away like you’ve never wanted anything.

sections.title: Inventory Is Memory
text: The parchment is a rough map drawn by an impatient hand.
Pinned to it is a small charm—an ink-black token stamped with a spiral mark.
It feels warm, like it remembers your grip.

lines: You pocket it. The air relaxes slightly, like a problem set aside.
You leave the charm where it is. The spiral mark watches you anyway.

sections.title: People Remember
text: You find a lantern-lit alcove where a lone sentry sits behind a simple wooden barricade.
Their armor is mismatched. Their eyes are sharp. Their patience is decorative.
They look you over the way someone inspects a story opening.

lines: Their shoulders drop a fraction. 'Right. You’re one of the ones who finishes things.'
Their mouth tightens. 'I won’t ask what you did. The look tells me enough.'
The sentry’s gaze snaps to the spiral mark. '…Ah.' Like they’ve just remembered a debt.
The sentry studies you longer than necessary. That’s how you know it was necessary.

sections.title: The World Pauses
text: Beyond the barricade is a stairwell spiraling down into softer darkness.
A faint door stands ajar at the bottom—inviting in the way a mouth invites.

lines: You move before the moment cools. The hinge doesn’t complain.

choices.text: Descend slowly. Listen for movement first.

lines: The moment has… cooled. The door is still there, but it feels less interested in being kind.

sections.title: The First Mirror
text: Inside is a small chamber with a mirror made of smoky glass.
Your reflection looks like you—mostly. The edges of it hesitate, like they’re checking rules.
A thin line of script appears across the glass, writing itself as you breathe.

lines: The script reads: 'You notice patterns. Good. The world hates repeating itself without supervision.'
The script reads: 'Brave. Simple. Efficient. The stone approves.'
A second line appears: 'Ah. You’re shaping yourself the hard way.'
A second line appears, then smudges itself out. You get the sense it was going to be unhelpful anyway.
The glass is warm. Not from heat—from attention.

choices.text: Turn away. Refuse the feeling of being measured.

lines: You don’t look back. The mirror does anyway.

sections.title: Health Is Not HP
text: Past the mirror is a narrow hall lined with shallow hooks.
One hook snags your sleeve—and for a split second, it feels like it snags something deeper than cloth.

lines: You move with the moment instead of against it. The hook slips free, disappointed.
You pull the wrong way. The hook catches and tugs—hard.
Your HP drops like a bruise. But your Health… your Health feels the insult and remembers it.
HP returns. Health changes you. The world will treat you differently when you’re worn thin.

choices.text: Wrap your injured hand (use a bandage if you have one).

lines: You wrap the wound. It’s practical. The world respects practical.
Respect is not the same as kindness.
You keep going. You’ve met worse. The world is taking notes.

sections.title: The World Gets Tired
text: The final chamber is simple: a stone arch, an ink-dark threshold, and a floor covered in old footprints.
Some prints run. Some stop.
A voice—quiet, bored, ancient—seems to speak from the arch itself:
'Stories don’t like to wait forever.'

choices.text: Hesitate at the threshold. Look for a better answer.
Try to force the arch to explain itself.

lines: The threshold does not argue. It simply becomes less interested in cooperating.

sections.title: ENDING A — The World Accepts You
text: You step through the threshold and the air changes—thicker, warmer, like a room that’s been waiting for someone who actually arrives.
The arch does not close behind you. That, somehow, is the kindness.
A thin line of ink writes itself across the stone at your feet:
'You may stay. That is not permission given lightly.'
For the first time since you woke, you feel the world stop measuring you long enough to simply… hold you.

lines: (The world accepts you. Tutorial complete.)

sections.title: ENDING B — The World Moves On
text: You wait for the threshold to soften. It doesn’t.
The arch remains, patient in the way stones are patient with rain.
Your breath feels louder than it should. Your thoughts feel smaller than they were a moment ago.
A thin line of ink appears across the stone—brief, clean, final:
'The story finishes. You are no longer required.'
There is no villain. No punishment speech. The world simply completes itself without you.
And you understand, too late, that being unnecessary is its own kind of ending.

lines: (The world moves on. Tutorial complete.)
